56fOepEAAAAJ.keywords: Data Structures, Algebraic Algorithms, Convex Optimization, Linear Programming, Interior Point Methods, Minimum-Cost Flow, Bipartite Matching, Dynamic Graph Algorithms, Dynamic Matrix Algorithms, Spectral Graph Sparsification
56fOepEAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Jan van den Brand is a faculty researcher affiliated with **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a research agenda and group working at the intersection of **algorithms**, *data structures*, and *optimization*. His work focuses on the design and analysis of fast exact and approximate algorithms for fundamental problems in combinatorial optimization and linear algebra, with particular emphasis on <u>linear programming</u>, <u>minimum-cost flow</u>, and <u>dynamic matrix and graph algorithms</u>. He combines tools from **algebraic algorithms**, **convex optimization**, and advanced data-structural techniques to obtain state-of-the-art runtimes and deterministic or near-deterministic guarantees.

---

## Research Areas
Van den Brand’s research spans several tightly connected areas: the development of fast solvers for convex programs (notably interior-point and primal–dual methods for linear programs), nearly-linear-time algorithms for flows and matchings, and the design of robust algebraic data structures that maintain matrix- and graph-related quantities under updates. Representative topics include deterministic and randomized linear-program solvers (e.g., “A deterministic linear program solver in current matrix multiplication time”), near-linear algorithms for minimum-cost flow and related problems (e.g., “Minimum cost flows, MDPs, and ℓ1-regression in nearly linear time for dense instances”), fast algorithms for bipartite matching on moderately dense graphs, and dynamic linear-algebraic primitives such as the dynamic matrix inverse (e.g., “Dynamic matrix inverse: Improved algorithms and matching conditional lower bounds”). He also explores applications of these techniques beyond classical combinatorial problems — for example, fast training approaches for overparameterized neural networks and algorithmic questions about attention in large language models — demonstrating a practice of unifying algebraic, combinatorial, and convex-optimization perspectives across both static and dynamic settings.

## Notable Works
- “A deterministic linear program solver in current matrix multiplication time” (2020) — Introduced a deterministic interior-point style solver that leverages fast matrix operations to push linear-program solving runtimes toward the current matrix-multiplication barrier, substantially advancing the theory of exact LP solvers.  
- “Minimum cost flows, MDPs, and ℓ1-regression in nearly linear time for dense instances” (2021) — Developed randomized algorithms that achieve improved runtimes for minimum-cost flow and related optimization problems on dense instances, bridging convex-programming techniques and flow-specific structure.  
- “Bipartite matching in nearly-linear time on moderately dense graphs” (2020) — Gave a nearly-linear-time randomized algorithm for maximum cardinality bipartite matching in the moderately dense regime, with broader implications for transshipment, optimal transport, and negative-weight shortest paths.

## Academic Background
Jan van den Brand’s doctoral thesis, “Dynamic Matrix Algorithms and Applications in Convex and Combinatorial Optimization” (2021), crystallizes a line of work on maintaining algebraic structures under updates and applying these primitives to dynamic graph and optimization problems. Since then he has been affiliated with **Georgia Tech**, building a research program that has produced highly cited contributions across algorithms, data structures, and optimization (total citations ≈ 1,537). His publication record includes influential papers on interior-point methods, dynamic algebraic data structures, and near-linear-time combinatorial algorithms, published in leading theory outlets and attracting substantial attention from the algorithms community. Van den Brand’s work is recognized for blending rigorous worst-case guarantees with practically motivated algorithmic primitives, and for producing deterministic and randomized breakthroughs that have reshaped the landscape of fast algorithms for linear algebra and combinatorial optimization.

5HJzWMUAAAAJ.keywords: Mathematics and Computer Science, Random graphs, Spin systems, Random constraint satisfaction problems, Approximate counting and sampling, Phase transitions and information-theoretic thresholds, Belief propagation and cavity method, Polymer models and cluster expansion, Markov chains and mixing times, Independent sets and extremal combinatorics
5HJzWMUAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Will Perkins is a professor at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads an active research group at the intersection of **mathematics**, **theoretical computer science**, and *statistical physics*. His work focuses on rigorous analysis of probabilistic combinatorics and random structures, with particular emphasis on the mathematical foundations of <u>information-theoretic and algorithmic thresholds</u>, <u>approximate counting and sampling</u>, and the geometry of high-dimensional solution spaces. Perkins' research program blends combinatorial methods, probabilistic techniques, and ideas from the physics-inspired cavity and replica methods to understand when inference and optimization problems on random graphs and constraint systems are tractable or computationally hard.

---

## Research Areas
Perkins' research spans several tightly related areas: the theory of random constraint satisfaction problems and planted models (e.g., random k-SAT and planted-SAT ensembles), rigorous validation and application of the physics-derived <u>cavity method</u> and *replica symmetry* predictions, and algorithmic approaches to approximate counting and sampling in spin and hard-core models. He has developed tools for analyzing spectral and information-theoretic thresholds (as in the stochastic block and bipartite block models), and for bridging abstract statistical-physics frameworks (Pirogov–Sinai, polymer models, cluster expansion) with efficient algorithms for sampling and counting on lattices, expanders, and random regular graphs. Other recurring themes in his papers include the study of independent sets and matchings (occupancy fractions, container approaches), low- and high-temperature behavior of Potts and Ising models, spatial mixing and uniqueness of Gibbs measures, and geometric and combinatorial questions such as sphere packings and kissing numbers in high dimensions. Across these topics he combines probabilistic combinatorics, complex-analytic and cluster-expansion techniques, and algorithmic analyses (FPTAS, Markov chains, and perfect sampling).

---

## Notable Works
- "Information-theoretic thresholds from the cavity method" (2018) — established rigorous formulas for mutual information in a broad family of inference problems induced by random graphs, vindicating the non-rigorous *cavity method* from physics and pinpointing <u>information-theoretic thresholds</u> in stochastic block-type models.  
- "On the complexity of random satisfiability problems with planted solutions" (2018) — identified and formalized large *algorithmic gaps* in planted k-SAT models, characterizing regimes where planted assignments are information-theoretically recoverable but algorithmically elusive.  
- "Algorithmic Pirogov–Sinai theory" (2020) — developed an efficient, general algorithmic framework using contour representations from Pirogov–Sinai theory to obtain approximate counting and sampling algorithms in the low-temperature regime for a broad class of lattice spin systems.

---

## Academic Background
Perkins' publication record and citation impact (over **2,100** citations) reflect a sustained research program at the confluence of probability, combinatorics, and theoretical computer science. He holds a faculty appointment at Georgia Tech and collaborates widely with researchers in mathematics, computer science, and statistical physics. His body of work includes influential contributions to rigorous probability and algorithm design for statistical-mechanical models, random graphs, and constraint satisfaction problems, and has led to algorithmic advances (FPTAS and efficient samplers), new analytic tools for Gibbs measures, and resolution of conjectures and thresholds predicted by physics. Perkins' work is supported by a combination of interdisciplinary grants and collaborations, and he is active in mentoring students and postdocs, organizing programs and workshops that bridge the mathematical and computational aspects of random structures and statistical mechanics.
